---
title: "Methods"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
---

```{=html}
<div class="masthead">
  <span>Electoral Atlas · Spanish Second Republic · 1931–1936</span>
  <div class="lang-switcher">
    <a href="../en/methods.html" class="lang-btn active">EN</a>
    <a href="../es/methods.html" class="lang-btn">ES</a>
    <a href="../ca/methods.html" class="lang-btn">CA</a>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="page-header">
  <div class="page-header-flag"></div>
  <div class="page-header-label">Behind the data</div>
  <h1 class="page-header-title">How We Built the Dataset</h1>
  <p class="page-header-sub">Sources, procedures, and decisions behind the atlas</p>
</div>

<section class="content-section">

  <!-- OVERVIEW -->
  <div class="section-label">Overview</div>
  <h2 class="section-title">From archives to atlas</h2>
  <p class="section-body">
    Building a consistent, municipality-level dataset for three elections held nearly a century
    ago required combining sources of very different kinds: printed provincial gazettes, archival
    records held at the Archivo Histórico Nacional, digitised copies of the <em>Gaceta de Madrid</em>,
    and regional archives across Spain's fifty provinces.
  </p>
  <p class="section-body">
    This page documents the main steps of that process — from identifying sources and transcribing
    raw vote counts to harmonising municipal boundaries and validating the final figures.
  </p>

  <!-- PROJECT HISTORY -->
  <div class="section-label mt-lg">Project history</div>
  <h3 class="section-title" style="font-size:1.3rem;">How we got here</h3>
  <div class="timeline">
    <div class="timeline-item">
      <div class="timeline-year">2021</div>
      <div class="timeline-text">Project conceived as part of a broader research programme on the political economy of interwar Europe.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="timeline-item">
      <div class="timeline-year">2022</div>
      <div class="timeline-text">First archival data collection phase: provincial capitals and large municipalities digitised from the <em>Gaceta de Madrid</em>.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="timeline-item">
      <div class="timeline-year">2023</div>
      <div class="timeline-text">Funding secured. Data collection expanded to all 50 provinces. Research assistant hired.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="timeline-item">
      <div class="timeline-year">2024</div>
      <div class="timeline-text">Beta version of the atlas launched. Codebook and methodology paper submitted for peer review.</div>
    </div>
    <div class="timeline-item">
      <div class="timeline-year">2025</div>
      <div class="timeline-text">Public release of the full dataset and this interactive atlas.</div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- DATA SOURCES -->
  <div class="section-label mt-lg">Data sources</div>
  <h3 class="section-title" style="font-size:1.3rem;">Where the numbers come from</h3>
  <p class="section-body">
    Election results were reported at the district (<em>circunscripción</em>) level in official publications,
    but partial municipal breakdowns were recorded in provincial diputación records, local press,
    and in the handwritten ledgers deposited at the Archivo Histórico Nacional. We combined
    all three to maximise geographic coverage.
  </p>
  <p class="section-body">
    Municipal boundaries are derived from the 1930 Census cartography and have been
    harmonised to allow comparisons across election years, accounting for municipal mergers
    and splits that occurred during the Republic.
  </p>

  <!-- IMAGE GALLERY PLACEHOLDER -->
  <div class="section-label mt-lg">Source examples</div>
  <h3 class="section-title" style="font-size:1.3rem;">What the originals look like</h3>
  <p class="section-body">
    Below are examples of the primary sources used in the data collection process.
    <!-- Replace the placeholder boxes below with actual <img> tags once images are ready -->
  </p>

  <div class="methods-gallery">
    <figure class="methods-figure">
      <!-- <img src="../images/methods/gaceta-example.jpg" alt="Gaceta de Madrid, 1931 election results"> -->
      <div class="methods-img-placeholder">Image placeholder — e.g. Gaceta de Madrid (1931)</div>
      <figcaption>Official results published in the <em>Gaceta de Madrid</em>, June 1931.</figcaption>
    </figure>
    <figure class="methods-figure">
      <!-- <img src="../images/methods/archive-ledger.jpg" alt="Archival ledger, provincial records"> -->
      <div class="methods-img-placeholder">Image placeholder — e.g. Provincial archive ledger</div>
      <figcaption>Handwritten vote tallies from a provincial diputación archive.</figcaption>
    </figure>
    <figure class="methods-figure">
      <!-- <img src="../images/methods/coding-sheet.jpg" alt="Data coding sheet"> -->
      <div class="methods-img-placeholder">Image placeholder — e.g. Data coding sheet</div>
      <figcaption>Standardised coding sheet used during data entry.</figcaption>
    </figure>
  </div>

  <!-- CODING DECISIONS -->
  <div class="section-label mt-lg">Coding decisions</div>
  <h3 class="section-title" style="font-size:1.3rem;">Key choices and limitations</h3>
  <p class="section-body">
    Party labels were standardised across all three elections using a consistent classification
    scheme based on the ECPR party families framework and adapted for the Spanish context.
    Where a coalition ran jointly, votes were allocated to the leading party unless a breakdown
    was available.
  </p>
  <p class="section-body">
    Turnout figures are based on the registered electorate as recorded in the 1930 Census.
    Municipalities for which no results could be recovered are indicated as missing in the dataset
    and appear grey on the map.
  </p>
  <p class="section-body">
    A full description of every variable, its source, and the decisions made during coding is
    available in the <a href="dataset.html">Dataset</a> tab.
  </p>

</section>

<footer class="site-footer">
  <p>Electoral Atlas of the Spanish Second Republic &nbsp;·&nbsp; Universitat Pompeu Fabra &nbsp;·&nbsp; 2025</p>
</footer>
```
